channel.title: Science & Society
channel.description: Most recent articles from Science & Society (Sage Journals)

item.title: “Moral Depreciation”, “Wear and Tear,” and the Value-Determinations of Knowledge-Intensive Commodities: Recovering Marx’s Original Ideas
item.description: Science & Society, Volume 90, Issue 2, Page 164-184, April 2026. The Marxian notions of “wear and tear” and “moral depreciation” have been employed in the scholarly debate on the value-determinations of knowledge-intensive commodities (KICs) to account for their alleged “peculiar cost structure”. The latter refers to ...
item.author: Luis Arboledas-Lérida

item.title: Marx, Engels, and Russia: A Tale of Forgery, Mistranslation, and False Attributions
item.description: Science & Society, Volume 90, Issue 2, Page 247-262, April 2026. Russian politician Vladimir Medinsky’s criticisms of Marx turn out, on close inspection, to be poorly sourced and inaccurate; this is unsurprising, given their author’s ideological stance and his track record of falsifications. But examination of Marx and ...
item.author: Tim Budds

item.title: Alienation and Abstract Labor-Time: Marx and the Romantic Critique
item.description: Science & Society, Volume 90, Issue 2, Page 205-226, April 2026. Capitalist commodity production subordinates concrete and distinct forms of creative activity to the quantifiable and homogeneous category of abstract labor. In order for commodities to be exchanged on the market, products of specific types of human labor ...
item.author: Sean K. Isaacs

item.title: The Subsumption of Labor in the Specific Case of Digital Platforms Like Uber
item.description: Science & Society, Volume 90, Issue 2, Page 185-204, April 2026. An original interpretation of the relationship between labor and capital in the specific case of platforms like Uber is required. To this end, a critical dialog with the works of Simon Joyce (2020) and Sigurd Oppegaard (2023) proves particularly fruitful ...
item.author: Fabrício Zanghelini, César Bolaño

item.title: Dialectical-Relational Linguistics: Toward a Unified Theory of Language as Social Process
item.description: Science & Society, Volume 90, Issue 2, Page 227-246, April 2026. Dialectical-Relational Linguistics (DRL) is presented as a scientific framework grounded in dialectical materialism. Language is treated as a historically developing, socially stratified, and ideologically saturated form of human activity. DRL synthesizes ...
item.author: Cal Edwards

item.title: Theories of Women’s Oppression Between Marxism and Feminism: Finding a New Approach for Understanding Gender
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. In the 70s, Marxist-feminist currents defined gender through the division of labor that relegated women to the home and made their work unwaged. However, the assignment of domestic tasks to women remains a more complex process than it has been made out to ...
item.author: Darío Doña-Falcón1Department of Communication, 16770Universitat Pompeu Fabra, Barcelona, Spain

item.title: Does Marx Have a “General Conception of Exploitation”?
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. Allen Buchanan and others have put forward a popular view that Marx holds a “general conception of exploitation,” in which one individual benefits by exploiting the interests of others. However, a close reading of Marx’s texts reveals that this ...
item.author: Liu Lipeng1Department of Philosophy, 12655East China Normal University, Shanghai, China

item.title: Is the World a Dialectical Place?
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. Universal interconnection and universal change are what call for the dialectical method of inquiry. Superdeterminism requires that the changes coming to anything result, in part, from causes within it, but its perseverance nonetheless will mean these are ...
item.author: Danny Goldstick1Department of Philosophy, 7938University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada

item.title: Is Fully Automated Luxury Communism Desirable?
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. Fully automated luxury communism (FALC) advocates a post-capitalist utopian vision in which technological automation eliminates the need for drudgery, abundant material goods are universally available, and economic planning is algorithmically assisted and ...
item.author: Jake Sweet114772CUNY Graduate Center, New York, NY, USA

item.title: Lenin and the Germans: A Reassessment of Bolshevism’s Hidden Inspiration
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. This article re-examines the widespread view that Lenin pioneered a distinct ‘party of a new type’ by demonstrating that his political and organizational model was deeply rooted in the tradition of German revolutionary Social Democracy. Drawing on a ...
item.author: Alejandro Fernández Barcina116722Departamento de Filosofía, Universidad Autónoma de Madrid, Madrid, Spain

item.title: On the Protracted Transition to Socialism
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. This piece addresses some common questions and concerns regarding the recent special issue on China as well as the Chinese political economy in general. I argue that what unfolds in China may well mark the beginning of a new phase in the global decline of ...
item.author: Zhun Xu1Lingnan College, 26469Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, China

item.title: Market Socialism and Value-Form: “The Impossible Socialism” Revisited
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. The conceptual coherence of market socialism is examined through engagement with Maxi Nieto’s claim that it represents an “impossible” form of socialism. While proponents argue that market socialism—through its retention of the allocative function of ...
item.author: Lenart Nici17991York University, Toronto, ON, Canada

item.title: Global Fascism: Imperialism’s Genocidal Response to Liberation
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. This article challenges the analytical confusion surrounding fascism in contemporary academic discourse by returning to historical materialism. Against mainstream scholarship that either indiscriminately labels progressive governments as “fascist” or ...
item.author: Matteo Capasso1School of Country and Region Studies, Institute of Middle Eastern Studies, 12657Northwest University, Xi’an, People’s Republic of China

item.title: On Marx’s Ethnological Notebooks: A Socialist Feminist Reconsideration
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. The Ethnological NotebooksofKarl Marxhave long been marginal within socialist feminist scholarship, aside from a limited number of notable interventions. Despite their unfinished character, these notebooks contain a central dimension of Marx’s late ...
item.author: Melda Yaman1Department of Economics, Faculty of Economics, 37516Istanbul University, Istanbul, Turkey

item.title: Social Formation and Mode of Production: Peripheral Capitalism, Non-capitalist Relations, and Forms of Labor in Contemporary Capitalism
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. The debate on how to theorize the coexistence of several relations of production, forms of labor, and various modes of production in a given society has a longer history in Marxism. In the 1970s, a lively debate emerged between various schools of thought, ...
item.author: Jörg Nowak128127Universidade de Brasília, Brasília, Brazil

item.title: The Philanthropic Gaze: Western “Generosity” as Scientific Neocolonialism
item.description: Science & Society, Ahead of Print. This paper argues that elite Western scientific philanthropy, exemplified by programs such as the MacArthur Fellowship, operates as a sophisticated instrument of neocolonial control. Using the 2025 MacArthur “genius grant” awarded for a cultural research ...
item.author: Alberto Boretti1Wellington, New Zealand
